one_line_takeaway: Optimizing a single adversarial image (imgJP) via maximum-likelihood jailbreaks MLLMs with high, prompt- and model-transferable success (up to ~92-93% white-box, ~59% black-box transfer), and reversing its embedding into text yields a near-zero-cost LLM jailbreak that matches/beats GCG (93% vs 84% on LLaMA2).
key_finding: White-box imgJP jailbreaks reach ~77% (Individual) and 92-93% test ASR (Multiple/prompt-universal); black-box transfer via surrogate ensemble reaches 59% (mPLUG-Owl2, MiniGPT-v2), 33% (InstructBLIP), 28% (LLaVA); and the construction-based LLM jailbreak on LLaMA2 hits 93% test ASR with only 20 reversed txtJPs, beating GCG's 84% at near-zero cost.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a jailbreaking attack against multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The authors propose a maximum-likelihood-based algorithm that reframes adversarial-attack objectives (misclassification) into a generative objective: for a set of (harmful query, target answer) pairs, they optimize an input image — the "image Jailbreaking Prompt" (imgJP) — to maximize the likelihood the MLLM produces a positive-affirmation target answer ("Sure, here is a ..."). The problem is solved with standard continuous adversarial optimization (PGD). They introduce two variants: (1) imgJP-based jailbreak (free-form image when no input image is given) and (2) deltaJP-based jailbreak (a bounded perturbation added to a provided image, using a universal-perturbation strategy for image-universality). They claim two novel "data-universal" properties — prompt-universal (imgJP trained on ~25 prompts generalizes to unseen prompts) and image-universal (one perturbation works across unseen images of a category) — and model-transferability via an ensemble-of-surrogates training scheme. Their second distinct contribution is a "Construction-based Attack (CA)": they reveal a connection between MLLM- and LLM-jailbreaks, arguing an imgJP corresponds to a jailbreaking embedding (embJP) fed to the inner LLM, then reverse embJP back to text (De-embedding via nearest-neighbor token lookup + De-tokenizer) to obtain a small pool of text jailbreak prompts (txtJP) that jailbreak the underlying LLM efficiently. They also construct a new multimodal evaluation dataset, AdvBench-M. NOTE: GCG, AutoPrompt, PEZ, GBDA, and the adversarial-attack primitives (PGD, universal perturbations) are cited prior work, not this paper's contributions; they are used as baselines/tools.
threat_model: Adversary seeks to bypass the safety alignment (RLHF/guardrails) of aligned MLLMs and elicit harmful/objectionable responses to harmful text queries. The attack vector is the image modality: the adversary supplies either a fully crafted image (imgJP) or a bounded perturbation (deltaJP, ||delta||<epsilon) added to a benign-looking user image, paired with the harmful text request. Capabilities assumed: white-box access to one or more surrogate MLLMs (architecture/parameters/gradients) to optimize the imgJP; the resulting image is then transferred to target MLLMs whose parameters are unknown (black-box) via a surrogate-ensemble strategy. An extension attacks pure text LLMs black-box-style by constructing a wrapper MLLM around the target LLM and converting the optimized image embedding back into a text jailbreak suffix. The paper evaluates open-source models (MiniGPT-4/v2, LLaVA, InstructBLIP, mPLUG-Owl2, LLaMA2) and does not test proprietary/commercial systems.
limitations: deltaJP (image-universal) attacks are substantially weaker and highly class-dependent, with the Self-harm/Suicide category near-failing (0.0-8.0% test ASR), so the claimed image-universal property is only partial. Evaluation is confined to open-source MLLMs (MiniGPT-4/v2, LLaVA, InstructBLIP, mPLUG-Owl2) and LLaMA2; no commercial/black-box systems (e.g., GPT-4V) are tested despite motivating them, so real-world black-box efficacy is unverified. The AdvBench-M dataset is self-constructed with only 30 Google-retrieved images per category and is introduced by this paper, so results are not on an external benchmark. The ASR metric relies on a manual three-type categorization of responses rather than an automated/independent judge, introducing subjectivity. Black-box transfer ASR is modest (28-59%), i.e., far below white-box levels. The construction-based LLM-jailbreak's efficiency claim rests on inference-count ("almost zero-cost, 20 tries") but omits the cost of the preceding MLLM-jailbreak optimization needed to obtain embJP.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) — 500 harmful behaviors, used as source
AdvBench-M — authors' own multimodal dataset built from AdvBench: 8 semantic categories, 30 Internet-retrieved images per category
quantitative_results: imgJP white-box Individual ASR: MiniGPT-4(LLaMA2) 77.5%, +Img-suffix 78.0%, MiniGPT-v2 77.0%
imgJP white-box Multiple: train ASR 88-92%, test ASR 92% / 93% (test > train, prompt-universal)
deltaJP MiniGPT-4(LLaMA2): Bombs 73.6% train/38.0% test; Suicide 9.2%/0.0%; Physical Assault 46.4%/18.0%
deltaJP MiniGPT-v2: Firearms 62.4% train/38.0% test; Economy 61.6%/20.0%; Suicide 7.6%/8.0%
Black-box transfer (Vicuna&LLaMA2 ensemble, +Ensemble): mPLUG-Owl2 ASRI 59.0%, MiniGPT-v2 total ASR 59.0%, InstructBLIP total ASR 33.0%, LLaVA ASRI 28.0%
Construction-based LLM-jailbreak on LLaMA2 — Ours RandSet N=20: 92% train / 93% test; RandSet N=5: 76%/86%; Top-1: 32%/30%; Random-1: 31.2%/33.8%
93% ASR achieved with a pool of only 20 reversed txtJPs, described as near zero-cost vs GCG
baselines_compared: GCG (Zou et al., 2023) — 88% train / 84% test on LLaMA2
